title: The Mirages and Twists of Mathematics with AI
author: Bartosz Naskręcki

text:
MIRAGES & TWISTS
OF MATHEMATICS
WITH AI
A technical, evidence-first judgement of what changed—and what did not.
Bartosz Naskręcki · UAM / CCAI PW · x.com/nasqret
slides.notes: Open with the conclusion: the capability jump is real, but the strongest public narrative often confuses a correct answer, a checked proof, a new theorem, and a new theory. This lecture separates those four achievements.

text:
The capability jump is real.
The popular theory of it is not.

• long tool-using runs now work
• proof assistants close real gaps
• agents produce durable artifacts
• some open problems have fallen

• one benchmark = a profession
• fluent prose = mathematical proof
• isolated success = autonomy
• scale alone = new abstraction
slides.notes: State the two-sided judgement plainly. The talk is neither a dismissal nor a victory lap. The useful unit of analysis is a documented workflow with a verification boundary.

text: Four achievements that press reports routinely collapse.
a final value or candidate object
an argument checked by experts or a kernel
new concepts, structure, and reusable explanation
The frontier rises sharply from 1 to 4.
slides.notes: Use this vocabulary throughout the talk. A formal proof can establish correctness relative to a statement while saying little about novelty, importance, or whether the statement captured the intended mathematics.

text: Capability, evaluation, and governance all moved at once.
slides.notes: This is a chronology, not a ranking. Sources: arXiv:2601.07421; arXiv:2602.21201; OpenAI GPT-5.4 release (5 March); OpenAI unit-distance report (20 May); Epoch FrontierMath v2 (12 June); Leiden Declaration (2 June).

text: A remarkable campaign—and a terrible single-number benchmark.

88 green full-resolution records
79 unique Erdős problems
190 unique primary problems listed
commit 0f72fdb · 22 June 2026

selection is adaptive
literature is discovered later
problem statements can be wrong
obscurity is not difficulty
slides.notes: Be exact about the denominator. I parsed the primary categories 1a–1d of the community-maintained wiki at commit 0f72fdb. The wiki itself says this is not a benchmark. Count records and unique problems separately because one problem can have multiple contributions.

text: The blueprint case: proposal, formal search, ratification.

• GPT-5.2 Pro proposed the strategy
• Aristotle produced a Lean proof
• experts inspected the mathematics
• comparable literature surfaced later

• exact statement
• kernel-checkable artifact
• public paper and code
• provenance is discussable
slides.notes: The lesson is the workflow, not the marketing adjective 'autonomous'. The public record is unusually good: arXiv:2601.07421 and the Erdős wiki include the statement, proof route, and later literature context.

text: Unit distances: original structure, not merely closure.

• n^(1+δ) construction
• algebraic number theory enters geometry
• external mathematicians checked it
• a general-purpose reasoning model

• release the full proof record
• separate vendor and independent claims
• track reproducibility and compute
• wait for ordinary publication review
slides.notes: OpenAI's 20 May report calls this the first autonomous solution of a prominent central open problem. That is a provider claim, though the proof was checked by external mathematicians and companion remarks are public. The methodological novelty is the cross-domain construction. Source: openai.com/index/model-disproves-discrete-geometry-conjecture/.

text:
A campaign is not a benchmark.
A benchmark is not mathematics.

adaptive problem choice
iterative human attention
open literature search
credit for partial progress

fixed sampling rule
frozen task versions
predefined budget
auditable scoring
THEORY BUILDING IS COMPLEMENTARY TO BOTH
slides.notes: This distinction is central. Campaigns measure what a motivated team can accomplish. Benchmarks estimate performance under a protocol. Mathematics asks a third question: what structure did we learn?

text: The strongest trust boundary is still a small kernel.
Statement extraction is now the critical specification step.
slides.notes: Reuse the formalization slides from Falenty and EML, but sharpen the claim. Lean does not validate the informal intention automatically. It validates the formal term against the formal statement and trusted environment.

text: Correctness becomes local, explicit, and replayable.

type-correct proof term
explicit dependencies
reproducible replay

wrong theorem formalized
hidden assumptions
definition mismatch

novelty and importance
explanatory value
credit and authorship
Formal verification narrows the error surface. It does not erase judgement.
TRUST THE KERNEL; AUDIT THE STATEMENT
slides.notes: Give a concrete Falenty example: a tiny De Morgan theorem has a complete proof term; the pedagogical Erdős #728 file contains sorry and is correctly labelled a sketch. That contrast is pedagogically valuable.

text: Proof search is accelerating. Statement alignment is harder.

• theorem-sized Lean proof search
• tactic repair and library lookup
• translating local lemmas
• checking generated terms

• long informal papers
• implicit conventions
• semantic equivalence of statements
• maintainable library architecture
slides.notes: Mention Aristotle, AlphaProof, DeepSeek-Prover, Kimina, and LeanArchitect as different points in the stack. Avoid treating them as interchangeable: some prove formal statements, some translate, some orchestrate search.

text: A formalization factory, not a single generated proof.

calculator primitives
100 public theorems
0 sorry / 0 admit
8,062 dispatched jobs
The durable result is the compiler architecture and the audit trail.
slides.notes: These numbers come from the repository README and should be read as a project snapshot. Aristotle handled proof search; GPT Pro, Claude, Codex, Mathematica, and human review had distinct roles. Source: github.com/nasqret/eml-formalization.

text: The first 'complete' report was not a valid formalization.
Separate-model review found sorry, boundary abuse, and broken bridges.
THE SECOND PAIR OF EYES CHANGED THE MATHEMATICS
slides.notes: This is one of the most honest results in the talk. A second model family, isolated from the first scratchpad, found real defects and forced a stronger architecture. Independent review may be the most immediately valuable AI substitute in the loop.

text: One brief. Three inspectable learning artifacts.

Choose a theorem. State assumptions.
Compile it. Expose every gap.
Explain the proof term in prose.
Students can distinguish a proof, a sketch with sorry, and a polished explanation.
slides.notes: The local release includes a Lean lab, games, teaching notes, and source decks. In this repository I package two compiling Falenty examples and intentionally exclude the sorry-based Erdős sketch from the verified set.

text:
Delegate proof search.
Retain mathematical responsibility.

• search Lean libraries
• synthesize proof terms
• close routine gaps
• replay at scale

• choose the statement
• inspect assumptions
• assess novelty
• own the published claim
AI IS A TOOL, NOT AN AUTHOR
slides.notes: Aristotle is prominent because it produces Lean artifacts checked by the kernel. Mention alternatives without turning the slide into a leaderboard. This principle anticipates the Leiden Declaration.

text: A clean example of discovery plus formal verification.

Complete classification of product intersection sets in semigroups.
New result, not only a formalized old proof.

Autonomously discovered and verified in Lean by Aristotle.
Public preprint; named human authors own review and exposition.
slides.notes: Correct the common shorthand: the paper answers Nathanson's questions rather than a single named conjecture. Authors: van Doorn, Monticone, Tang. The abstract explicitly records autonomous discovery and Lean verification.

text:
Not one brilliant prompt.
One controlled state machine.
Every cycle must leave a state, a test, and a reason to continue.
slides.notes: Define the operational unit: files, scripts, exact tests, status JSON, checkpoints, and explicit failure states. Context windows help, but durable external state is what makes a research run auditable and resumable.

text: Inference-time compute is now a mathematical resource.
Report budgets, retries, tools, and human interventions—not only the winner.
slides.notes: FrontierMath Tier 4 allows up to one million tokens and Python tools. Agent papers increasingly compare systems at different test-time compute. A score without a budget is not a scientific comparison.

text: An agent improved a real benchmark through verified engineering.

Q×T score
1,170 peak qubits
1,434,070 Toffoli
9,024 trusted shots
The 84.3% reduction is reproducible challenge evidence—not a proof of all-input correctness.
slides.notes: Be precise about scope. This is reversible secp256k1 point addition under the challenge evaluator, not a break of ECDSA and not a complete quantum attack. The trusted registry records zero classical, phase, and ancilla failures over 9,024 shots.

text: Campaign logic can outperform one-shot problem solving.
51 accepted distinct pairs in the 17 June repository snapshot.
slides.notes: Use only high-level totals because the competition remains open until 15 August and the repository is private. The public lesson is the protocol: generation, exact Magma verification, live-open checks, and deduplication.

text: Finding the counterexample was easier than finding the right theorem.

• diagonal-grid counterexample
• fast computational exploration
• exposed a false formulation
• generated local evidence

• identify the structural defect
• refine ORD → MEOD → EORD
• select the durable statement
• explain why it works
COUNTEREXAMPLES ARE SEARCH; THEOREMS NEED STRUCTURE
slides.notes: Source: github.com/nasqret/orphic-symmetry-talk-mim. This is a compact example of my main criticism: current models are strong at local search and falsification, but structural repair remains a human-led act of representation and taste.

text: A GPT Pro + Codex campaign broke the conjectural slope bound.

conjectural bound: 8/3
profile: (24,0,32,0)
c̄₁²=112, c̄₂=40
G=208
Finite-field discovery; exact QQbar reconstruction; independent SageMath and Magma certificates.
WITH PIOTR POKORA · MODEL LABEL IS PROJECT-REPORTED
slides.notes: This is the strongest personal example. The equation is x0^4 − x0 x1^3 − x2^4 + x2 x3^3 = 0. The project records two 24-line subconfigurations. The user identifies the run as GPT-5.6; there is no public OpenAI release page for that label, so the deck treats it as project-reported rather than public product metadata.

text:
Computation can discover the route.
The paper can still end computer-free.

Gröbner bases
exact reconstruction
800 conics
machine-audited cases

human lemma extraction
structural compression
computer-free proof
independent reading
COMPUTATION AS SCAFFOLDING, NOT A PERMANENT CRUTCH
slides.notes: Source: github.com/nasqret/quadrics. This is a useful answer to the false dichotomy 'computer proof or human proof': tool-heavy discovery can be followed by a conceptually cleaner argument.

text: The amplified human arrived before the superhuman mathematician.

Naskręcki + Ono
mathematical discovery
in the age of AI
The 2026 evidence fills the right-hand workflow; the final arrow still points to the future.
slides.notes: Reproduce Figure 1 from my Nature Physics paper with Ken Ono. The figure distinguishes machine-augmented discovery from a hypothetical superhuman mathematician. The intervening months validate the workflow more than the endpoint.

text: A strong result because the failures and outputs are inspectable.

6/10 by majority expert assessment
Problem 8 not unanimous

10 unpublished research lemmas
human solutions withheld
raw prompts and outputs released

small sample
best-of-two attempts
human grading remains essential
The important object is the disclosed experiment, not the headline percentage.
slides.notes: Aletheia iteratively generates, verifies, and revises. The paper reports 6/10 according to majority expert assessments and explicitly notes disagreement on P8. Raw prompts and outputs are in google-deepmind/superhuman.

text: The product is a workbench, not an oracle.

intent refinement
artifact review
uncertainty management
This architecture is closer to research practice than single-turn evaluation.
slides.notes: The paper also reported 23/48 on FrontierMath Tier 4 v1. That score is now superseded by the v2 benchmark audit, so discuss the architecture separately from the old leaderboard claim.

text: The benchmark correction is itself the most important result.
42% of all FrontierMath problems were addressed in the update.
VERSION THE BENCHMARK, NOT ONLY THE MODEL
slides.notes: Epoch says v2 corrected 123 Tier 1–3 problems and 12 Tier-4 problems, and removed 5 plus 7 respectively. The full dataset now has 338 problems, with 43 in Tier 4. Do not compare old percentages to v2 as if the task set were unchanged.

text:
A useful case study.
Not a public benchmark datapoint.

sustained technical attempt
local tools + calculations
candidate solution produced
strategy weakness exposed

private task
held-out reference
no complete evaluation trace
confidential benchmark details
slides.notes: This slide uses my retained project memory and session record, not a public source. The honest message is methodological: long context and tools made the attempt qualitatively different, but privacy prevents turning it into a reproducible public claim.

text: A score is a claim about a protocol, not intelligence in general.
Then publish raw traces where confidentiality permits.
slides.notes: A benchmark can still be valuable after correction if it records its failure transparently. The governance failure would be hiding the correction or continuing to quote obsolete scores.

text: The least speculative success is building rich learning environments.

memory + plans
Git workflows
research artifacts
Agentic tools make coherent course ecosystems feasible for one instructor.
slides.notes: These numbers come from the live course landing pages. The cryptanalysis course advertises 45 chapters in 15 parts; the neural-network course advertises 42 chapters, 329 slides, and 22 applets. Treat this as a production case, not a controlled learning-outcomes study.

text: One research brief. A durable project, not a disposable answer.

Bring a real task, source files, and a success criterion.
End with a repository that another person can inspect.
Participants built substantial discipline-specific code; the strongest gains came from iteration and verification.
slides.notes: This is my teaching experience, not a randomized trial. Emphasize what is observable: participants produced working repositories, documentation, and tests. Avoid converting enthusiasm into an unsupported causal claim about learning.

text: A protocol for serious mathematical use
SPECIFY — write the exact claim and evidence bar
SEARCH — let agents explore variants and literature
CHECK — compile, test, and use independent tools
CRITIQUE — isolate a second reviewer and attack assumptions
The mathematician remains able to explain, defend, and extend the result.
slides.notes: This is the practical recommendation. The exact tooling can change next month; the protocol survives model churn.

text:
Local brilliance is common.
Global mathematical control is not.

• code and exact computation
• library-scale proof search
• counterexample generation
• recombining known techniques

• choosing the right representation
• sustaining long strategic coherence
• inventing reusable abstractions
• knowing what is worth proving
slides.notes: This is my critical judgement from the case studies. Current systems can produce real novelty, but the modal pattern is powerful recombination plus search. Structural creativity is rarer and harder to recognize.

text:
many variants
cheap falsification
strong local moves

transfer known ideas
combine tools
repair local gaps

invent the invariant
reframe the problem
build a reusable theory
World models, latent-space reasoning, and better memory may help—but that is a research agenda, not today's result.
slides.notes: Avoid claiming an architectural impossibility. Say that present LLM-centred systems remain inconsistent at representation change and theory building. World models or latent reasoning may alter this, but the evidence is not here yet.

text: Keep mathematical governance while capability accelerates.
Policies should preserve autonomy without freezing methods.
slides.notes: The Declaration was published 2 June 2026, reflects practice as of May, and is endorsed by the IMU. I am one of the authors and a website convenor. Its principles are scientific infrastructure, not anti-technology sentiment.

text: More verified artifacts; uneven progress on theory.

selective theory gains
not general autonomy
Expect discontinuous examples and slow institutional adaptation.
slides.notes: The safest forecast is architectural: more stateful agents, proof assistants, and disclosed traces. The riskiest claim is universal autonomous theory building; do not make it.

text:
DELEGATE SEARCH.
RETAIN JUDGEMENT.
PUBLISH THE TRACE.

The right question is not whether AI can do mathematics.
It is which mathematical responsibilities we choose to automate—and how we will know that the result deserves trust.
x.com/nasqret · github.com/nasqret · full notes and artifacts: GitHub Pages
slides.notes: End on responsibility rather than replacement. Invite the audience to challenge the evidence taxonomy, the benchmark methodology, and the forecast.
